quiz.question: The food critic gave the restaurant a superb rating in her review. What does this tell us about the meal?
options: It was average and unremarkable
It was below expectations
It was outstanding and excellent
It was too expensive for what it offered
quiz.answer: It was outstanding and excellent
quiz.explanation:
Superb means of the highest quality — excellent, magnificent.
• superb = impressively excellent
• From Latin: 'superbus' — proud, magnificent
• A superb performance, a superb view — always the highest praise

quiz.question: The government introduced new laws to curb excessive spending. What were the laws designed to do?
options: Encourage more spending
Measure and track spending levels
Restrain and limit spending
Redistribute spending to other areas
quiz.answer: Restrain and limit spending
quiz.explanation:
To curb means to restrain or keep under control.
• curb = to keep in check; to limit or restrain
• As a noun: the edge of a pavement/sidewalk — both senses involve 'containing'
• 'Curb your enthusiasm' = hold it back

quiz.question: Before buying the novel, she read the blurb on the back cover. What was she reading?
options: The first chapter of the book
A short promotional description of the book's content
A list of the author's other works, prizes, and biographical details
A collection of reviews from professional critics and academics
quiz.answer: A short promotional description of the book's content
quiz.explanation:
A blurb is a short promotional piece of writing, typically on the back of a book.
• blurb = a brief, enthusiastic description designed to attract buyers
• Coined by American humorist Gelett Burgess in 1907
• Also used for any short publicity write-up

quiz.question: The wildlife photographer captured images of a lion cub in the savanna. What was in her photographs?
options: A fully grown male lion
A young, juvenile lion
A female lion on a hunt
An elderly, weakened lion
quiz.answer: A young, juvenile lion
quiz.explanation:
A cub is a young animal, especially of bears, lions, wolves, or foxes.
• cub = the young offspring of certain wild animals
• Also: a 'Cub Scout' (a young scout) — the youth connotation is consistent
• From Old Norse: 'kobbi' — young seal

quiz.question: At the gala, the celebrity walked past her former co-star without a word or glance — a deliberate snub. What did she do?
options: She greeted him warmly but briefly before moving on to greet others
She intentionally ignored and showed contempt for him
She genuinely failed to recognize him because of the crowd and low lighting
She introduced him to other guests at the event
quiz.answer: She intentionally ignored and showed contempt for him
quiz.explanation:
To snub means to rebuff or ignore someone in a deliberately contemptuous way.
• snub = a deliberate cold shoulder; an intentional slight
• From Old Norse: 'snubba' — to scold, rebuke
• The key is intent — accidentally missing someone is not a snub

quiz.question: The preacher was accused of being pharisaic — lecturing others about charity while giving nothing himself. What was being criticized?
options: His lack of biblical knowledge and poor sermon preparation
His hypocrisy in appearing pious while not practicing what he preached
His strict and devout religious practice, which alienated younger members
His overly simple sermons that lacked theological depth
quiz.answer: His hypocrisy in appearing pious while not practicing what he preached
quiz.explanation:
Pharisaic means self-righteously pious and hypocritical — observing the form of religion without its spirit.
• pharisaic = sanctimonious and hypocritical in religious matters
• From the Pharisees — a Jewish sect criticized in the Gospels for rigid rule-following
• 'Pharisaic piety' = outward show of holiness masking inner selfishness

quiz.question: The artist created a mosaic portrait of the city's founder for the town hall. How was the portrait made?
options: By oil painting on a large canvas stretched over a wooden frame
From tiny pieces of colored glass, stone, or tile fitted together
By printing a high-resolution photograph directly onto a ceramic surface
Through a digital design printed on the wall using industrial printers
quiz.answer: From tiny pieces of colored glass, stone, or tile fitted together
quiz.explanation:
A mosaic is a picture or pattern made from small pieces of colored glass, stone, or tile.
• mosaic = an artwork composed of many small, fitted pieces
• From Latin/Greek: via 'Musa' — of the Muses (art)
• Also figurative: 'a mosaic of cultures' = many distinct elements forming a whole

quiz.question: The travel writer complained that the new resort was prosaic compared to the wild landscape it had replaced. What was wrong with it?
options: It was dangerous and had been built to unsafe standards throughout
It was dull, ordinary, and lacking any imagination or beauty
It was overpriced for what guests actually received during their stay
It was too remote and inaccessible for the majority of tourists to reach
quiz.answer: It was dull, ordinary, and lacking any imagination or beauty
quiz.explanation:
Prosaic means lacking imagination or excitement; dull and ordinary.
• prosaic = commonplace, unromantic, matter-of-fact
• From Latin: 'prosa' — prose (as opposed to the elevated language of poetry)
• 'A prosaic explanation' = a flat, unimaginative one

quiz.question: Sprinting 100 meters is an anaerobic exercise. What does this tell us about how the body powers the sprint?
options: The muscles rely heavily on breathing in oxygen during the effort
The muscles produce energy without using oxygen
The exercise is specifically good for lung capacity
The body uses fat as its primary fuel source
quiz.answer: The muscles produce energy without using oxygen
quiz.explanation:
Anaerobic means occurring without oxygen — the opposite of aerobic.
• anaerobic = not requiring oxygen for energy production
• From Greek: 'an-' (without) + 'aer' (air) + 'bios' (life)
• Short, intense bursts (sprints, weight lifting) are anaerobic; sustained effort (jogging) is aerobic

quiz.question: The editor was known for her acerbic feedback — writers left her office shaken. What was her feedback like?
options: Encouraging, constructive, and full of specific praise for good sections
Sharp, harsh, and bitingly critical
Vague, non-committal, and ultimately very unhelpful to the writers
Gentle, diplomatic, and always careful not to hurt anyone's feelings
quiz.answer: Sharp, harsh, and bitingly critical
quiz.explanation:
Acerbic means sharp and forthright in a harsh or bitter way.
• acerbic = sharply and bitingly critical; caustic in tone
• From Latin: 'acerbus' — harsh, bitter (originally of taste)
• 'Acerbic wit' = humor that stings; not gentle or affectionate

quiz.question: The painter depicted the baby with a cherubic face. How did the baby appear in the painting?
options: Wise and serious beyond its years, with an alert, knowing expression
Chubby-cheeked, innocent, and adorably sweet
Mischievous and wild-eyed, suggesting a difficult and energetic temperament
Pale, sickly, and fragile-looking, as though unwell
quiz.answer: Chubby-cheeked, innocent, and adorably sweet
quiz.explanation:
Cherubic describes someone having a round, innocent, angelic face — like a cherub.
• cherubic = resembling a cherub; innocently sweet and rosy-faced
• Cherubs in art are depicted as chubby, rosy, angelic children
• From Hebrew: 'kerub' — a type of angel

quiz.question: The Mongolian herders lived a nomadic lifestyle. What does this tell us about where they lived?
options: They lived in a large permanent city with established trade routes
They moved from place to place with no fixed home
They lived on boats and traveled along the river systems throughout the year
They had settled in one village for many generations and rarely left
quiz.answer: They moved from place to place with no fixed home
quiz.explanation:
Nomadic describes a way of life involving constant movement rather than settling in one place.
• nomadic = roaming from place to place, without a permanent home
• From Greek: 'nomas' — roaming for pasture
• Modern use: 'a nomadic lifestyle' — traveling constantly, often for work

quiz.question: The patient reported sporadic chest pains over the past month. What pattern did the pains follow?
options: They occurred at regular, predictable intervals every few days
They were constant and completely unrelenting throughout the entire month
They occurred irregularly, at scattered, unpredictable times
They only happened during vigorous physical exercise or exertion
quiz.answer: They occurred irregularly, at scattered, unpredictable times
quiz.explanation:
Sporadic means occurring at irregular intervals; not continuous or systematic.
• sporadic = scattered, occasional, irregular
• From Greek: 'sporaden' — here and there, scattered
• 'Sporadic violence' = isolated outbreaks, not sustained conflict

quiz.question: The professor had encyclopedic knowledge of medieval history. What does this suggest about her knowledge?
options: She knew a few key facts in extraordinary depth and detail
She had broad, comprehensive knowledge covering a wide range of details
She had memorized one specific, well-regarded textbook cover to cover
Her knowledge was thorough but dated — she had not kept up with new research
quiz.answer: She had broad, comprehensive knowledge covering a wide range of details
quiz.explanation:
Encyclopedic means covering a wide range of subjects in great detail — like an encyclopedia.
• encyclopedic = comprehensive and wide-ranging in knowledge
• Does not mean 'deep in one area' — it implies breadth across many details
• 'An encyclopedic memory' = able to recall vast amounts of information

quiz.question: His attendance at meetings was spasmodic — sometimes three in a row, then nothing for weeks. What pattern is described?
options: Regular and reliable attendance at every single scheduled meeting
A gradual and steady decline in attendance over the course of the year
Sudden, irregular bursts of attendance with long gaps
A slow but consistent improvement in attendance as the months went on
quiz.answer: Sudden, irregular bursts of attendance with long gaps
quiz.explanation:
Spasmodic means occurring in sudden bursts at irregular intervals.
• spasmodic = happening in irregular spasms or bursts; fitful
• From Greek: 'spasmos' — convulsion
• Like muscle spasms — sudden, not sustained, unpredictable

quiz.question: The doctor described the patient's memory loss as episodic rather than constant. What does this mean?
options: The memory loss was permanent, total, and irreversible with no exceptions
It occurred in distinct, separate episodes rather than continuously
The memory loss was entirely linked to one specific traumatic past event only
The memory loss was gradually and measurably worsening over time
quiz.answer: It occurred in distinct, separate episodes rather than continuously
quiz.explanation:
Episodic means occurring in episodes — separate, distinct occurrences rather than continuously.
• episodic = happening as isolated episodes, not continuously
• From Greek: 'epeisodion' — a coming in besides; an inserted section
• 'Episodic memory' = memory of specific events (vs. general knowledge)

quiz.question: The prolific novelist published seventeen books in ten years. What does 'prolific' tell us about her output?
options: She produced very few but extremely well-crafted works
She produced an exceptionally large amount of work
She wrote only one genre of fiction
Her books were all short and quickly written
quiz.answer: She produced an exceptionally large amount of work
quiz.explanation:
Prolific means producing many works, results, or offspring — highly productive.
• prolific = producing a large quantity; highly fertile or creative
• From Latin: 'proles' (offspring) + 'facere' (to make)
• 'A prolific goal scorer', 'a prolific writer' — always about high output quantity

quiz.question: Witnesses described the accident as horrific. How did they feel watching it?
options: Mildly concerned but not particularly alarmed by what they saw
Deeply shocked and disturbed by something extremely terrible
Curious and fascinated, unable to look away from the unfolding scene
Confused and disoriented because they could not understand what had happened
quiz.answer: Deeply shocked and disturbed by something extremely terrible
quiz.explanation:
Horrific means causing horror — extremely shocking and disturbing.
• horrific = causing feelings of horror or shock; dreadful
• From Latin: 'horrificus' — causing dread
• Stronger than 'terrible' — implies visceral shock and distress

quiz.question: The monk sat in meditation with a beatific smile. What kind of smile was it?
options: A forced, polite smile maintained for the benefit of those around him
A blissfully serene and radiantly happy expression
A sly, knowing grin suggesting secret amusement at something private
A pained smile carefully concealing considerable discomfort and distress
quiz.answer: A blissfully serene and radiantly happy expression
quiz.explanation:
Beatific means blissfully happy — expressing supreme happiness or blessedness.
• beatific = showing or producing a feeling of supreme happiness
• From Latin: 'beatus' — blessed, happy
• 'A beatific vision' in theology = seeing God directly; 'beatification' = step toward sainthood

quiz.question: The two companies' synergic partnership produced results neither could have achieved alone. What does 'synergic' mean here?
options: Each company worked entirely independently and avoided all overlap
The combined effort created a result greater than the sum of their separate efforts
One company provided the capital while the other provided all of the labor
The companies entered into fierce competition to see which could outperform the other
quiz.answer: The combined effort created a result greater than the sum of their separate efforts
quiz.explanation:
Synergic (or synergistic) means working together to produce a combined effect greater than the sum of individual efforts.
• synergic = relating to synergy; cooperative interaction that boosts overall effect
• From Greek: 'synergos' — working together
• '1 + 1 = 3' is the classic synergy metaphor

quiz.question: The fashion editor called the designer's new collection effortlessly chic. What was she praising?
options: Its affordability and accessibility
Its elegance and stylish sophistication
Its bold and shocking provocation
Its use of traditional, classic materials
quiz.answer: Its elegance and stylish sophistication
quiz.explanation:
Chic means elegantly fashionable and sophisticated.
• chic = stylish in a refined, effortless way
• From French: 'chic' — elegance, style
• Implies understated elegance rather than flashy or try-hard fashion

quiz.question: The choirboy's seraphic voice reduced the congregation to tears. What quality did his voice have?
options: A powerful, thundering resonance
A heavenly, pure, and angelic beauty
A rough and emotionally raw quality
A technically precise, cold perfection
quiz.answer: A heavenly, pure, and angelic beauty
quiz.explanation:
Seraphic means characteristic of a seraph — pure, angelic, and heavenly.
• seraphic = angelically beautiful, blissfully serene, and pure
• Seraphim are the highest order of angels in Jewish and Christian tradition
• 'A seraphic smile' = innocent and radiantly joyful

quiz.question: The news broadcast warned viewers that the next footage contained graphic images. What should viewers expect?
options: Artistic, carefully composed imagery of the highest aesthetic quality
Vivid, disturbing images not suitable for sensitive viewers
Low-resolution, blurry archival footage that was hard to make out clearly
Animated explanatory diagrams illustrating the key facts of the story
quiz.answer: Vivid, disturbing images not suitable for sensitive viewers
quiz.explanation:
Graphic (in this context) means vividly explicit — especially of violence, injury, or disturbing content.
• graphic = clearly and vividly depicted, often disturbingly so
• From Greek: 'graphikos' — of writing or drawing
• 'Graphic violence' = depicted in explicit, disturbing detail

quiz.question: The archaeologist found hieroglyphic inscriptions on the tomb wall. What kind of writing did she find?
options: Latin script inscribed during the Roman occupation of the region
Ancient Egyptian picture-based writing symbols
An early form of the Greek alphabet used in the Hellenistic period
A form of Chinese calligraphy dating from the early Han dynasty
quiz.answer: Ancient Egyptian picture-based writing symbols
quiz.explanation:
Hieroglyphic refers to the ancient Egyptian writing system using pictorial symbols.
• hieroglyphic = relating to a system of writing using pictorial characters
• From Greek: 'hieros' (sacred) + 'glyphein' (to carve) — 'sacred carvings'
• Now used informally: 'I can't read your hieroglyphics' = illegible handwriting

quiz.question: Archaeologists discovered paleolithic tools at the dig site. Roughly when were these tools made?
options: About 500 years ago, during the early modern period of European history
Around 2,500 years ago, in the classical era of ancient Greece
Between roughly 2.5 million and 10,000 years ago, in the Old Stone Age
In the Bronze Age, approximately 3,000 to 4,000 years before the present
quiz.answer: Between roughly 2.5 million and 10,000 years ago, in the Old Stone Age
quiz.explanation:
Paleolithic means relating to the early Stone Age, characterized by chipped stone tools.
• paleolithic = of the Old Stone Age (the earliest period of human prehistory)
• From Greek: 'palaios' (ancient) + 'lithos' (stone)
• Contrast: Neolithic = the New Stone Age (polished stone tools, farming begins)

quiz.question: A neolithic settlement was uncovered, complete with grain storage pits. What does this tell us about the people who lived there?
options: They were nomadic hunter-gatherers who never built permanent structures
They lived in the New Stone Age and practiced farming and settled life
They were early Bronze Age metalworkers using copper and tin alloys
They belonged to a classical Greek colony established around 700 BCE
quiz.answer: They lived in the New Stone Age and practiced farming and settled life
quiz.explanation:
Neolithic means relating to the later Stone Age, when humans developed farming and settled communities.
• neolithic = of the New Stone Age; characterized by polished stone tools and agriculture
• From Greek: 'neos' (new) + 'lithos' (stone)
• Grain storage confirms farming — a defining neolithic trait

quiz.question: The doctor measured the baby's cephalic index. What was she measuring?
options: The baby's weight relative to its length on a standard growth chart
A ratio relating to the dimensions of the skull/head
The circumference of the baby's chest measured at the nipple line
The electrical activity of the baby's brain recorded during sleep
quiz.answer: A ratio relating to the dimensions of the skull/head
quiz.explanation:
Cephalic means relating to the head or skull.
• cephalic = of or relating to the head
• From Greek: 'kephale' — head
• 'Cephalic index' = ratio of skull width to length; used in physical anthropology

quiz.question: The villain's plan was described as diabolic in its cruelty. What does this tell us about the plan?
options: It was cleverly simple and efficient
It was fiendishly evil and cruel
It was well-intentioned but misguided
It was too complicated to ever work
quiz.answer: It was fiendishly evil and cruel
quiz.explanation:
Diabolic means characteristic of the devil — outrageously evil or cruel.
• diabolic = devilish; extremely wicked
• From Greek: 'diabolos' — the devil, the accuser
• 'A diabolic scheme' implies it is not just bad, but maliciously, viciously evil

quiz.question: The painting showed a bucolic scene: rolling meadows, grazing sheep, a quiet farmhouse. What kind of scene is this?
options: A dramatic, stormy urban landscape
A peaceful, idealized rural scene
A busy harvest scene full of laborers
A bleak, industrial countryside
quiz.answer: A peaceful, idealized rural scene
quiz.explanation:
Bucolic means relating to the pleasant aspects of rural life — charmingly pastoral.
• bucolic = of or relating to the pleasant, idealized countryside
• From Greek: 'boukolikos' — of a herdsman
• 'Bucolic tranquility' = the peaceful, simple life of the countryside

quiz.question: A critic praised the curator's catholic taste in art, noting that the exhibition covered everything from Renaissance masters to contemporary street art. What does 'catholic' mean here?
options: Religiously inspired art with direct connections to church doctrine
Art focused on a narrow, concentrated theme from a single tradition
A wide-ranging, all-inclusive appreciation with no narrow limits
Art that had received formal approval and endorsement from church authorities
quiz.answer: A wide-ranging, all-inclusive appreciation with no narrow limits
quiz.explanation:
With a lowercase 'c', catholic means universal, all-embracing, or wide-ranging in taste.
• catholic (lowercase) = broad-minded; covering or including much; universal
• From Greek: 'katholikos' — universal
• Distinct from 'Catholic' (uppercase) = relating to the Roman Catholic Church

quiz.question: The politician's vitriolic speech shocked even his own supporters. What was the tone of the speech?
options: Measured, calm, and carefully supported with evidence and data
Bitter, caustic, and savagely attacking
Emotional but ultimately optimistic and hopeful about the future
Dry, factual, and deliberately stripped of any personal feeling
quiz.answer: Bitter, caustic, and savagely attacking
quiz.explanation:
Vitriolic means filled with bitter, scathing criticism — like vitriol (sulfuric acid).
• vitriolic = savagely harsh and bitter in speech or writing
• From 'vitriol' — sulfuric acid, a highly corrosive substance
• 'A vitriolic attack' burns with bitterness and contempt

quiz.question: The museum displayed a collection of ceramic vessels from ancient Greece. What were the vessels made from?
options: Bronze and copper alloys worked by skilled craftsmen using hammers
Clay that has been shaped and hardened by heat
Stone that was carefully carved and polished into smooth vessel shapes
Woven plant fibers coated with resin to form a watertight surface
quiz.answer: Clay that has been shaped and hardened by heat
quiz.explanation:
Ceramic refers to objects made from clay and hardened by firing at high temperatures.
• ceramic = made from clay, fired to harden it
• From Greek: 'keramos' — potter's clay
• Ceramics include pottery, porcelain, tiles, and bricks

quiz.question: The hotel advertised panoramic views from every room. What would guests see from their windows?
options: A partial view of the garden, framed by the surrounding architecture
A wide, sweeping, unobstructed view in all directions
A view restricted to the hotel's own landscaped interior courtyard
A view framed tightly on both sides by rows of closely planted trees
quiz.answer: A wide, sweeping, unobstructed view in all directions
quiz.explanation:
Panoramic means giving a wide, complete view of an extensive area.
• panoramic = offering a sweeping, all-encompassing view
• From Greek: 'pan' (all) + 'horama' (view)
• 'A panoramic photograph' = a wide-angle image capturing the full scene

quiz.question: Health officials declared a flu epidemic in the city. What does this indicate?
options: A single isolated confirmed case of flu was being investigated
Flu was always present at consistently low background levels
Flu cases had spread rapidly and widely within the city's population
Flu had simultaneously spread to every country and territory on Earth
quiz.answer: Flu cases had spread rapidly and widely within the city's population
quiz.explanation:
An epidemic is a widespread outbreak of a disease within a specific community or region.
• epidemic = a disease spreading rapidly through a specific population
• From Greek: 'epi' (upon) + 'demos' (people)
• Contrast: endemic (always present locally), pandemic (global spread)

quiz.question: When COVID-19 was declared a pandemic, what had changed from being merely an epidemic?
options: The disease had suddenly become more lethal in its newest variant
The outbreak had spread across multiple countries, affecting the whole world
Several dangerous new variants had been discovered in quick succession
The official death toll had passed a specific internationally agreed threshold
quiz.answer: The outbreak had spread across multiple countries, affecting the whole world
quiz.explanation:
A pandemic is a disease outbreak occurring across a very wide area — multiple countries or worldwide.
• pandemic = an epidemic that has spread globally
• From Greek: 'pan' (all) + 'demos' (people)
• Key distinction from epidemic: scale — epidemic = regional, pandemic = global

quiz.question: Malaria is endemic to many tropical regions. What does this mean?
options: Malaria has been completely eradicated from those regions
Malaria regularly occurs there as a permanent, expected presence
Malaria only appears there during extreme weather events each year
Malaria is spreading rapidly from those regions toward a global pandemic
quiz.answer: Malaria regularly occurs there as a permanent, expected presence
quiz.explanation:
Endemic means habitually present in a particular area — a persistent, ongoing presence rather than an outbreak.
• endemic = native to or regularly found in a specific region
• From Greek: 'en' (in) + 'demos' (people)
• Also used for species: 'The kiwi is endemic to New Zealand' = found only there

quiz.question: The pamphlet was a polemic against industrial farming. What kind of writing was it?
options: A carefully balanced, peer-reviewed scientific assessment of farming methods
A strongly argued, combative attack on a position or practice
A personal memoir about the author's own experiences growing up on a farm
An official government policy document outlining proposed new regulations
quiz.answer: A strongly argued, combative attack on a position or practice
quiz.explanation:
A polemic is a strong verbal or written attack on a particular opinion, doctrine, or person.
• polemic = a controversial argument; a piece of writing attacking a position
• From Greek: 'polemikos' — warlike, hostile
• A polemicist does not seek balance — they argue one side forcefully

quiz.question: Critics called the film's plot anemic. What were they saying about it?
options: Overly complex, confusing, and impossible to follow without prior knowledge
Thin, weak, and lacking in substance
Too predictable, following the genre formula without any deviation
Derivative — clearly copying the style and structure of earlier, better films
quiz.answer: Thin, weak, and lacking in substance
quiz.explanation:
Anemic used figuratively means lacking in vitality, weak, or thin.
• anemic = (figuratively) lacking strength, color, or vigor
• From the medical condition 'anemia' — insufficient red blood cells, causing weakness
• 'An anemic performance' = weak, lifeless, unconvincing

quiz.question: The cardiologist noted that the patient's heartbeat was arrhythmic. What concerned her?
options: The heart was beating too fast but maintaining a perfectly steady pattern
The heart's rhythm was irregular and not beating in a normal pattern
The heart was beating too slowly but with a completely consistent rhythm
The heart showed clear signs of structural enlargement on the scan
quiz.answer: The heart's rhythm was irregular and not beating in a normal pattern
quiz.explanation:
Arrhythmic means lacking a regular rhythm — especially of the heartbeat.
• arrhythmic = having an irregular, abnormal rhythm
• From Greek: 'a-' (without) + 'rhythmos' (rhythm)
• Cardiac arrhythmia = a group of conditions where the heart beats irregularly

quiz.question: The director wanted the scene to be comic rather than dramatic. How should the actors play it?
options: With intense, emotional seriousness
In a way intended to provoke laughter
In a tragic, somber manner
As realistically and quietly as possible
quiz.answer: In a way intended to provoke laughter
quiz.explanation:
Comic means relating to or causing comedy — intended to amuse.
• comic = causing amusement; humorous
• From Greek: 'komikos' — of comedy
• 'Comic timing', 'comic effect' — always connected to humor, not drama

quiz.question: The philosopher's gnomic reply — 'The answer is always in the question' — left the students puzzled. What characterized his reply?
options: It was a lengthy, detailed philosophical explanation covering multiple points
It was brief, pithy, and cryptically wise
It was a blunt and direct refusal to engage with the question at all
It was a light-hearted joke designed to make the students laugh and relax
quiz.answer: It was brief, pithy, and cryptically wise
quiz.explanation:
Gnomic means expressed in short, cryptic sayings that sound wise but are hard to interpret.
• gnomic = using or containing short, memorable, often obscure statements of wisdom
• From Greek: 'gnome' — maxim, judgment
• A gnomic utterance is like a riddle wrapped in wisdom

quiz.question: The nurse prepared a hypodermic needle to administer the vaccine. Where would the injection go?
options: Directly and deeply into a major vein for rapid absorption
Just beneath the surface of the skin
Deep into a large muscle group such as the gluteus or deltoid
Orally, swallowed as a liquid passing down through the stomach
quiz.answer: Just beneath the surface of the skin
quiz.explanation:
Hypodermic means relating to the area just beneath the skin.
• hypodermic = under the skin
• From Greek: 'hypo' (under) + 'derma' (skin)
• A hypodermic injection delivers medicine just below the skin layer

quiz.question: Scientists detected seismic activity near the volcano. What were their instruments picking up?
options: Unusual and rapid changes in the prevailing wind patterns
Vibrations and tremors moving through the ground
A measurable increase in volcanic gas emissions into the surrounding air
A steady rise in the surface temperature of the rock around the caldera
quiz.answer: Vibrations and tremors moving through the ground
quiz.explanation:
Seismic relates to earthquakes or vibrations through the earth.
• seismic = of or relating to earthquakes or ground vibrations
• From Greek: 'seismos' — shaking
• Figurative: 'a seismic shift in policy' = an enormously significant change

quiz.question: The philosopher asked: 'What is our cosmic significance?' What scale was she questioning?
options: The importance of individual humans within their own nation and culture
Human significance in the context of the entire universe
The proper role of science and reason in contemporary modern society
The long-term future of Earth and its biosphere over the next thousand years
quiz.answer: Human significance in the context of the entire universe
quiz.explanation:
Cosmic means relating to the universe — of unimaginably vast scale.
• cosmic = of or relating to the cosmos (the universe as a whole)
• From Greek: 'kosmos' — order, universe
• 'Cosmic rays', 'cosmic scale', 'cosmic significance' — all imply the universe-level

quiz.question: The community described the cult leader's influence as satanic. What were they implying?
options: He was a deeply religious man who followed strict rules
His influence was pure evil and diabolically corrupting
He was simply very strict and demanding
He was involved in unusual but harmless rituals
quiz.answer: His influence was pure evil and diabolically corrupting
quiz.explanation:
Satanic means of or relating to Satan — supremely evil, diabolical.
• satanic = characteristic of the devil; profoundly wicked
• From 'Satan' — the embodiment of evil in Abrahamic religions
• Used hyperbolically: 'a satanic plan' = one of monstrous evil

quiz.question: The construction project required a titanic effort from the entire workforce. How large was the effort required?
options: A modest but sustained daily contribution from individual workers
An effort of enormous, almost superhuman scale
A carefully phased series of small, precisely coordinated steps over years
A single decisive burst of energy concentrated over one intense final week
quiz.answer: An effort of enormous, almost superhuman scale
quiz.explanation:
Titanic means of exceptional strength, scale, or importance — like the Titans.
• titanic = gigantic; of enormous power or scale
• From the Titans of Greek mythology — beings of immense power
• Note: 'Titanic' (uppercase) = the famous ship; 'titanic' (lowercase) = the adjective

quiz.question: The scientists stored the biological samples using cryogenic techniques. What did this involve?
options: Exposing samples to very high temperatures in order to fully sterilize them
Storing samples at extremely low temperatures to preserve them
Chemically fixing and embedding the samples permanently in clear resin
Removing all moisture by freeze-drying the samples inside a vacuum chamber
quiz.answer: Storing samples at extremely low temperatures to preserve them
quiz.explanation:
Cryogenic means relating to very low temperatures — used for freezing and preservation.
• cryogenic = of or relating to the production and effects of very low temperatures
• From Greek: 'kryos' (cold, frost) + '-genic' (producing)
• Cryogenic storage is used for cells, embryos, and even human bodies ('cryonics')

quiz.question: The early 20th-century government pursued eugenic policies, deciding who could and could not have children. What was the aim of such policies?
options: Providing better healthcare and nutrition for all newborn children
Controlling reproduction to supposedly improve population genetics
Encouraging higher birth rates among the poorest and most deprived communities
Improving educational outcomes and social mobility for disadvantaged groups
quiz.answer: Controlling reproduction to supposedly improve population genetics
quiz.explanation:
Eugenic means relating to eugenics — the practice of attempting to 'improve' a population through selective breeding.
• eugenic = relating to the controlled reproduction of humans to increase 'desirable' traits
• From Greek: 'eu-' (good, well) + 'genos' (race, birth)
• Eugenics was used to justify horrific abuses and is now widely condemned

quiz.question: The census recorded the ethnic composition of the country. What was it measuring?
options: The declared religious beliefs and denominational affiliations of the population
The groupings based on shared cultural, national, or ancestral background
The distribution of income levels and economic classes across different regions
The specific languages and regional dialects spoken by each household
quiz.answer: The groupings based on shared cultural, national, or ancestral background
quiz.explanation:
Ethnic means relating to a group of people sharing a common cultural, national, tribal, or ancestral heritage.
• ethnic = of or relating to a human group sharing distinctive cultural, racial, or national traits
• From Greek: 'ethnos' — nation, people
• 'Ethnic food', 'ethnic minority', 'ethnic identity'

quiz.question: When asked for his opinion of the 400-page report, the general's laconic reply was: 'Fix it.' What does this tell us about his communication style?
options: He used long, elaborate sentences full of nuance
He communicated using very few words
He refused to give any feedback at all
He preferred to write rather than speak
quiz.answer: He communicated using very few words
quiz.explanation:
Laconic means using very few words — brief to the point of curtness.
• laconic = expressing much in few words; terse
• From 'Laconia' (Sparta) — Spartans were famously brief in speech
• Classic example: when Philip II threatened Sparta ('If I enter Laconia, I will raze it'), Sparta's reply was: 'If.'

quiz.question: The psychologist described the subject's hedonic baseline — the level of pleasure at which he normally operated. What aspect of his life was being measured?
options: His intellectual capacity and academic performance across various subjects
His typical level of pleasure, enjoyment, and well-being
His capacity to endure and tolerate pain, discomfort, and suffering
His patterns of moral and ethical decision-making in difficult situations
quiz.answer: His typical level of pleasure, enjoyment, and well-being
quiz.explanation:
Hedonic means relating to pleasure — the pursuit of happiness or enjoyable sensations.
• hedonic = of or relating to pleasure; concerned with pleasurable experience
• From Greek: 'hedone' — pleasure
• 'Hedonic treadmill' = the tendency to return to a stable level of happiness after events

quiz.question: When told she had to wait fifteen minutes, the customer became histrionic — weeping and shouting. What does 'histrionic' mean here?
options: She reacted with calm, measured frustration and asked to speak to a manager
She reacted in an excessively theatrical, overdramatic way
She quietly and patiently explained her grievance to the member of staff
She became withdrawn and silent, refusing to engage with anyone present
quiz.answer: She reacted in an excessively theatrical, overdramatic way
quiz.explanation:
Histrionic means excessively theatrical or melodramatic in behavior.
• histrionic = overly dramatic; performing emotions in an exaggerated way
• From Latin: 'histrio' — actor
• Histrionic behavior is about performance: the reaction is out of proportion to the situation

quiz.question: The patient had chronic back pain. How long had she likely been suffering?
options: For only a few days following the initial injury before fully subsiding
During a brief but extremely intense period that then resolved completely
For a long time — months or years — on an ongoing basis
Repeatedly, in isolated bursts lasting only a few minutes at a time
quiz.answer: For a long time — months or years — on an ongoing basis
quiz.explanation:
Chronic means persisting for a long time or constantly recurring — not acute or brief.
• chronic = long-lasting, persistent (of an illness or condition)
• From Greek: 'chronos' — time
• Opposite: acute (severe and short-lived). 'Chronic' implies duration, not necessarily severity

quiz.question: She insisted that her relationship with her male colleague was purely platonic. What was she clarifying?
options: Their friendship was based on a shared passion for philosophy and ideas
There was nothing romantic or sexual in their relationship — just friendship
They had a complicated history that had once included a romantic involvement
She admired him greatly from a distance but had never personally spoken to him
quiz.answer: There was nothing romantic or sexual in their relationship — just friendship
quiz.explanation:
Platonic means intimate and affectionate but not sexual or romantic.
• platonic = of close friendship without romantic or physical elements
• From Plato — who wrote about a pure, spiritual form of love ('Platonic love')
• 'Platonic friendship' = deep but non-romantic

quiz.question: The startup was still in its embryonic stage. What does this tell us about the company?
options: It was well-established, growing steadily, and posting consistent profits
It was just beginning and not yet fully formed or developed
It had recently undergone a major internal restructuring and rebranding
It was in advanced talks and close to being acquired by a much larger firm
quiz.answer: It was just beginning and not yet fully formed or developed
quiz.explanation:
Embryonic means in an early, undeveloped stage — like an embryo before it has fully formed.
• embryonic = in the very early stages of development
• From Greek: 'embryon' — fetus
• 'Embryonic ideas', 'embryonic technology' — just beginning, not yet mature

quiz.question: Scholars struggled to decode the runic inscriptions on the ancient stone. What kind of writing were they?
options: Ancient Latin script inscribed during the Roman occupation of northern Europe
Characters from the ancient alphabet used by Germanic and Norse peoples
Oracle bone script from the Shang dynasty used in early Chinese divination
Symbols derived from the ancient Phoenician alphabet that spread westward
quiz.answer: Characters from the ancient alphabet used by Germanic and Norse peoples
quiz.explanation:
Runic means relating to runes — the characters of the alphabet used by ancient Germanic and Norse peoples.
• runic = of or relating to runes; often mysterious or magical in connotation
• From Old Norse: 'run' — secret, mystery
• Runes were used for writing, divination, and inscriptions on objects

quiz.question: When the politician announced new pledges, the cynic in the crowd muttered, 'He'll never follow through.' What characterizes a cynic's worldview?
options: A tendency to readily trust the stated good intentions of others
A belief that self-interest drives all, and things disappoint
An unusual ability to see the best possible outcome in any situation
A detailed scholarly knowledge of political history and institutions
quiz.answer: A belief that self-interest drives all, and things disappoint
quiz.explanation:
A cynic is someone who distrusts others' motives and expects the worst.
• cynic = a person who believes people act from selfish motives and doubts sincerity
• From the ancient Cynics — Greek philosophers who rejected social conventions
• 'Cynical' = distrustful of sincerity; scornful of idealism

quiz.question: The journalist described the festival as a kaleidoscopic mix of color, sound, and culture. What was she conveying?
options: The festival had a simple, uniform look and feel throughout
The festival was a vivid, constantly shifting blend of varied elements
The festival focused solely on one specific regional cultural tradition
The festival was chaotically disorganized, with no clear program or structure
quiz.answer: The festival was a vivid, constantly shifting blend of varied elements
quiz.explanation:
Kaleidoscopic means constantly and colorfully shifting — made up of many vivid, changing elements.
• kaleidoscopic = complex and varied, like the patterns seen in a kaleidoscope
• From Greek: 'kalos' (beautiful) + 'eidos' (form) + 'skopein' (to look at)
• Implies rich variety and constant, beautiful change

quiz.question: The biologist said the contamination was caused by microscopic organisms. What does this tell us about the organisms?
options: They were small but still visible to the naked eye
They were too small to see without a microscope
They were small enough to pass through a standard filter
They were invisible even under a microscope
quiz.answer: They were too small to see without a microscope
quiz.explanation:
Microscopic means too small to be seen by the naked eye — requiring a microscope.
• microscopic = extremely small; visible only with microscopic magnification
• From Greek: 'mikros' (small) + 'skopein' (to look at)
• Figurative: 'microscopic detail' = an extremely fine level of scrutiny

quiz.question: The billionaire was known for his philanthropic activities. What did he do with his wealth?
options: He invested it aggressively in emerging markets
He donated it to causes that benefited humanity
He established a luxury empire
He used it to fund political campaigns
quiz.answer: He donated it to causes that benefited humanity
quiz.explanation:
Philanthropic means charitable — showing a desire to promote the welfare of others.
• philanthropic = devoted to helping others; generous and charitable
• From Greek: 'philos' (loving) + 'anthropos' (human being)
• 'Philanthropist' = a person who donates to humanitarian causes

quiz.question: The CEO was criticized for her myopic strategy that focused only on next quarter's profit. What was the flaw in her thinking?
options: She was excessively focused on long-term sustainability at the cost of profit
She could only see immediate short-term gains and lacked broader vision
She was balancing too many competing strategic priorities simultaneously
She had completely ignored shareholder interests in her three-year plan
quiz.answer: She could only see immediate short-term gains and lacked broader vision
quiz.explanation:
Myopic means short-sighted — both literally (unable to see far) and figuratively (lacking foresight).
• myopic = lacking imagination, foresight, or a long-term view
• From Greek: 'myein' (to close the eyes) + 'ops' (eye)
• Mirrors the eye condition: a myopic eye can only focus on what is near

quiz.question: His choleric temperament meant that even minor delays at the airport sent him into a rage. What does 'choleric' describe?
options: A calm and easygoing nature
A tendency to anger quickly and easily
A deeply melancholic and sad disposition
An excessively optimistic outlook
quiz.answer: A tendency to anger quickly and easily
quiz.explanation:
Choleric means easily angered — having a hot-tempered, irritable personality.
• choleric = bad-tempered, easily provoked to anger
• From ancient humour theory: 'choler' (yellow bile) was the humour of anger
• The four temperaments: sanguine, choleric, melancholic, phlegmatic

quiz.question: The pharmacist offered a generic version of the medication at a lower price. What does 'generic' mean here?
options: A lower-quality substitute made using inferior active ingredients
A non-branded version containing the same active ingredient
An older formulation that had been officially discontinued by the manufacturer
A product originally designed and licensed for an entirely different condition
quiz.answer: A non-branded version containing the same active ingredient
quiz.explanation:
Generic means not having a brand name — a general product not marketed under a proprietary name.
• generic = not branded; belonging to a general class
• A generic drug has the same active compound as the branded version
• Figuratively: 'generic' = lacking distinctive qualities; standard and unspecific

quiz.question: The professor's lecture on esoteric branches of medieval philosophy drew only three students. Why so few?
options: The topic was far too basic — every student already knew it thoroughly
The topic was highly specialized, understood only by a few experts
The lecture had been awkwardly scheduled at a time nobody could make
The professor had a long-standing reputation for being a poor and dull teacher
quiz.answer: The topic was highly specialized, understood only by a few experts
quiz.explanation:
Esoteric means intended for or understood by only a small group with specialized knowledge.
• esoteric = understood by few; specialist and obscure
• From Greek: 'esoterikos' — inner, within (for insiders only)
• Opposite: exoteric (accessible to all)

quiz.question: The young musician's meteoric rise to fame surprised even her managers. What does 'meteoric' tell us about her rise?
options: It was steady and gradual over many years
It was sudden and extremely rapid
It was driven by a large marketing budget
It was limited to a small regional fanbase
quiz.answer: It was sudden and extremely rapid
quiz.explanation:
Meteoric means very rapid and sudden — like the brief, blazing trail of a meteor.
• meteoric = (of a career or rise) spectacularly fast and brilliant
• From 'meteor' — a space rock that burns brightly but briefly across the sky
• Note: a 'meteoric rise' borrows the speed but not the brevity of a meteor

quiz.question: The opposition accused the prime minister of relying on rhetoric rather than policy. What were they criticizing?
options: His inability to speak publicly in a clear, confident, and articulate way
His use of persuasive language that lacked substantive content
His deliberate strategy of avoiding all contact with the press
His failure to command a working majority in the chamber of parliament
quiz.answer: His use of persuasive language that lacked substantive content
quiz.explanation:
Rhetoric refers to the art of persuasive speaking — often used to imply style over substance.
• rhetoric = language designed to persuade; sometimes implying hollow or empty words
• From Greek: 'rhetorike' — the art of an orator
• 'Just rhetoric' = impressive words with no real meaning or action behind them

quiz.question: The cave paintings were prehistoric, predating any known writing system. What does this tell us about when they were made?
options: They were produced in ancient Egypt or Mesopotamia around 3,000 BCE
They were made before humans developed written records
They were created in the early medieval period, before the year 1000 CE
They were produced by the very first organized literate civilizations
quiz.answer: They were made before humans developed written records
quiz.explanation:
Prehistoric means belonging to the period before written historical records.
• prehistoric = relating to the time before written history
• From Latin: 'prae' (before) + 'historia' (written record)
• Prehistoric ≠ ancient history; it specifically means before any written accounts existed

quiz.question: The designer made the building's facade deliberately asymmetric. What does the facade look like?
options: It has perfectly balanced, identical halves on both sides of a central axis
The two sides are different and do not mirror each other
It follows a precise repeating geometric pattern all the way around
It appears perfectly identical and symmetrical when viewed from all four sides
quiz.answer: The two sides are different and do not mirror each other
quiz.explanation:
Asymmetric means lacking symmetry — the two sides are unequal or different.
• asymmetric = not identical on both sides; without symmetry
• From Greek: 'a-' (without) + 'symmetria' (equal measure)
• Asymmetric warfare = a conflict where the two sides have very unequal power

quiz.question: The target had five concentric rings. How were the rings arranged?
options: Stacked directly on top of one another in parallel horizontal rows
Arranged outward in a widening spiral from a single starting point
Nested inside each other, sharing the same center point
Placed side by side in a row with equal gaps of white space between them
quiz.answer: Nested inside each other, sharing the same center point
quiz.explanation:
Concentric means sharing the same center — circles or rings nested within each other.
• concentric = (of circles) having a common center
• From Latin: 'con-' (together) + 'centrum' (center)
• The classic example: ripples in water, or a target's rings

quiz.question: Her egocentric colleague interrupted every meeting to talk about his own achievements. What does 'egocentric' mean?
options: He was deeply insecure and constantly sought reassurance from his colleagues
He was self-centered, placing himself at the center of everything
He was highly ambitious and exclusively goal-focused in all his activities
He was introverted, reserved, and frequently awkward in large group settings
quiz.answer: He was self-centered, placing himself at the center of everything
quiz.explanation:
Egocentric means self-centered — thinking only from one's own perspective.
• egocentric = regarding oneself as the center of everything; excessively self-focused
• From Latin: 'ego' (I) + 'centrum' (center)
• Distinct from narcissism (admiring oneself); egocentrism is about perspective, not just vanity

quiz.question: The patient was admitted for gastric surgery. Which organ was the surgeon operating on?
options: The liver
The stomach
The small intestine
The gallbladder
quiz.answer: The stomach
quiz.explanation:
Gastric means relating to the stomach.
• gastric = of or relating to the stomach
• From Greek: 'gaster' — stomach
• 'Gastric acid', 'gastric band', 'gastric bypass' — all involve the stomach

quiz.question: The senator delivered a panegyric at the retiring general's farewell ceremony. What did she deliver?
options: A frank critical assessment of his career's most significant failures and regrets
An elaborate speech of glowing praise in his honor
A brief administrative announcement marking the end of his official posting
A formal eulogy delivered in honor of someone who had recently passed away
quiz.answer: An elaborate speech of glowing praise in his honor
quiz.explanation:
A panegyric is a public speech or writing offering elaborate praise of a person or achievement.
• panegyric = a formal public speech or text full of praise
• From Greek: 'panegyrikos' — of a public assembly
• Note: a panegyric is for the living; a eulogy is specifically for the dead

quiz.question: The doctor prescribed an analgesic for the patient's post-operative pain. What type of medication is this?
options: A medication prescribed specifically to help the patient sleep and rest
A medication that reduces or eliminates pain
A broad-spectrum antibiotic to prevent post-operative wound infections
A corticosteroid prescribed to reduce dangerous post-surgical inflammation
quiz.answer: A medication that reduces or eliminates pain
quiz.explanation:
An analgesic is a painkilling drug.
• analgesic = a substance that relieves pain without loss of consciousness
• From Greek: 'an-' (without) + 'algesis' (pain sensation)
• Examples: aspirin, ibuprofen, morphine — all analgesics of different strengths

quiz.question: The forensic team was called to examine the crime scene. What was their job?
options: To identify and formally arrest the prime suspects in the case
To apply scientific methods to gather and analyze evidence
To conduct detailed one-on-one interviews with all available witnesses
To write the official incident and preliminary investigation report
quiz.answer: To apply scientific methods to gather and analyze evidence
quiz.explanation:
Forensic means applying scientific methods to legal or criminal investigations.
• forensic = relating to the use of science in legal contexts
• From Latin: 'forensis' — of the forum (the public court)
• 'Forensic evidence' = scientifically collected and analyzed evidence

quiz.question: The psychologist noted that the student was driven by extrinsic motivation — she studied only to win prizes. What kind of motivation is extrinsic?
options: Motivation from a deep internal love and genuine curiosity for the subject
Motivation from external rewards or pressures outside the activity itself
Motivation that disappears the moment a major goal has been achieved
Motivation driven purely by rivalry and competition with peers
quiz.answer: Motivation from external rewards or pressures outside the activity itself
quiz.explanation:
Extrinsic means coming from outside — not part of the essential nature of something.
• extrinsic = external; originating from outside
• From Latin: 'extrinsecus' — from the outside
• Opposite: intrinsic = belonging to the essential nature of something ('intrinsic value')

quiz.question: When asked if she had any regrets, her answer was an emphatic 'No.' What does this tell us about her reply?
options: She answered with some hesitation, leaving a trace of ambiguity in her reply
She answered with force and absolute certainty, leaving no room for doubt
She politely declined to answer the personal question at all
She gave a long, thoughtful, nuanced answer exploring multiple perspectives
quiz.answer: She answered with force and absolute certainty, leaving no room for doubt
quiz.explanation:
Emphatic means expressing something forcefully and with no ambiguity.
• emphatic = clearly and strongly expressed; definitive
• From Greek: 'emphatikos' — significant, expressive
• 'An emphatic win' = a decisive, clear-cut victory

quiz.question: The engineer handed the trainee a schematic of the electrical system. What did the trainee receive?
options: A detailed photographic record of the finished, fully installed system
A simplified diagram showing how the system is organized and connected
A comprehensive written manual explaining how to safely operate the system
A full parts list itemizing every component needed to build the system
quiz.answer: A simplified diagram showing how the system is organized and connected
quiz.explanation:
A schematic is a simplified, symbolic diagram representing a system or structure.
• schematic = a diagram using symbols to represent the structure of a system
• From Greek: 'schema' — shape, form
• 'Schematic diagram' shows relationships, not realistic appearances

quiz.question: The literature professor organized the course thematically rather than chronologically. How was it organized?
options: Books were arranged in strict chronological order by publication date
Books were grouped by shared themes, ignoring when they were written
Books were sorted by the country or region in which they were published
Books were assigned in ascending order from shortest to longest
quiz.answer: Books were grouped by shared themes, ignoring when they were written
quiz.explanation:
Thematic means organized according to themes or subjects.
• thematic = relating to or based on themes
• From Greek: 'thema' — proposition, theme
• 'Thematic analysis' groups material by recurring ideas rather than by time or author

quiz.question: The Berlin Wall was described as emblematic of the Cold War. What does this mean?
options: It was the single most destructive and costly event of the entire Cold War
It was a perfect symbolic representation of the Cold War's divisions
It is widely regarded as the formal starting point of the Cold War period
It remained the Cold War's least well-known and most overlooked monument
quiz.answer: It was a perfect symbolic representation of the Cold War's divisions
quiz.explanation:
Emblematic means serving as a symbol or typical example of something.
• emblematic = symbolic of; a perfect representative case
• From 'emblem' — a symbolic image or badge
• 'Emblematic of the era' = a single thing that captures the whole spirit of a period

quiz.question: Faced with the housing crisis, the mayor took a pragmatic approach: rather than wait for a perfect solution, she approved temporary shelter immediately. What does 'pragmatic' mean here?
options: She applied a strict and consistent ideological framework to every decision
She dealt with the situation practically, based on what would actually work
She delayed taking any action until a full independent study had been completed
She ignored the housing crisis in favor of other items on her policy agenda
quiz.answer: She dealt with the situation practically, based on what would actually work
quiz.explanation:
Pragmatic means dealing with things in a practical, realistic way rather than according to fixed theory.
• pragmatic = concerned with practical results; sensible and realistic
• From Greek: 'pragmatikos' — skilled in business
• Opposite of idealistic or dogmatic — pragmatism prioritizes what works

quiz.question: When the office fire alarm went off — for the fourth time that month — the phlegmatic receptionist calmly picked up her coffee and walked out. What does 'phlegmatic' describe?
options: A quick temper and dramatic reactions
A calm, composed, unhurried temperament
A fearful and anxious personality
An excessively cheerful and sociable nature
quiz.answer: A calm, composed, unhurried temperament
quiz.explanation:
Phlegmatic means having a calm, stoic, and unemotional disposition.
• phlegmatic = calmly self-possessed; not easily excited or upset
• From the humour theory: 'phlegm' was the humour of calmness
• The four temperaments: sanguine (cheerful), choleric (angry), melancholic (sad), phlegmatic (calm)

quiz.question: The optometrist found that the patient was astigmatic. What is wrong with his vision?
options: He can see distant objects clearly but everything close up appears blurry
He has an irregularly shaped cornea that causes blurred vision at all distances
He experiences very poor vision in low light and complete darkness only
He cannot distinguish between certain pairs of colors, particularly red and green
quiz.answer: He has an irregularly shaped cornea that causes blurred vision at all distances
quiz.explanation:
Astigmatic means affected by astigmatism — a refractive error caused by an irregular cornea.
• astigmatic = having astigmatism; vision that is blurred due to irregular cornea shape
• From Greek: 'a-' (without) + 'stigma' (point) — the eye cannot focus to a single point
• Unlike myopia (near) or hyperopia (far), astigmatism blurs at all distances

quiz.question: When asked which of her two friends she preferred, Carla gave a diplomatic answer: 'You both bring something unique.' What made her answer diplomatic?
options: It was completely honest and direct, stating her genuine preference clearly
It was tactful and careful, avoiding offense to either party
It avoided the question entirely by suddenly changing the subject
It was curt and dismissive, ending the conversation as quickly as possible
quiz.answer: It was tactful and careful, avoiding offense to either party
quiz.explanation:
Diplomatic means tactful, skilled at handling sensitive situations without causing offense.
• diplomatic = showing sensitivity and skill in dealing with people
• From 'diplomat' — an official trained in negotiation and tact
• Diplomatic ≠ dishonest; it means carefully worded to preserve relationships

quiz.question: The chef scattered fresh herbs over the dish to make it more aromatic. What did the herbs add?
options: Additional texture and a satisfying crunch when eating the dish
A pleasant, distinctive fragrance
A stronger, more pronounced heat and spiciness to the overall flavor
A burst of bright visual color that improved the presentation of the dish
quiz.answer: A pleasant, distinctive fragrance
quiz.explanation:
Aromatic means having a pleasant, notable fragrance.
• aromatic = having a strong, pleasant smell
• From Greek: 'aroma' — spice, fragrance
• In chemistry: 'aromatic compounds' have a ring structure — a separate technical meaning

quiz.question: The musician played a chromatic scale. What does this tell us about the notes she played?
options: She played only the notes belonging to the standard major key of the piece
She played every semitone — all twelve notes within an octave
She improvised freely, playing notes in a completely random spontaneous order
She played exclusively the black keys on the piano, avoiding all white keys
quiz.answer: She played every semitone — all twelve notes within an octave
quiz.explanation:
Chromatic in music means involving all twelve semitones of the scale.
• chromatic scale = a scale moving by semitones, using all 12 notes in an octave
• From Greek: 'khroma' — color (the 'color' notes outside the diatonic scale)
• In visual art: 'chromatic' relates to color — both senses share the Greek root

quiz.question: The gallery displayed the achromatic photographs alongside colorful paintings. What did the photographs look like?
options: They were printed in unusually vibrant, saturated colors
They were in black and white, containing no color
They were tinted in a single warm hue
They were blurred and out of focus
quiz.answer: They were in black and white, containing no color
quiz.explanation:
Achromatic means without color — in tones of black, white, and gray only.
• achromatic = free of color; using only black, white, and gray
• From Greek: 'a-' (without) + 'khroma' (color)
• An achromatic lens (in optics) corrects color distortion

quiz.question: The interior designer created a monochromatic room using only shades of blue. What is the defining feature of a monochromatic scheme?
options: It uses no color at all — only black, white, and gray
It uses a single color in various shades and tints
It uses two complementary colors in equal proportion
It uses the full spectrum of rainbow colors
quiz.answer: It uses a single color in various shades and tints
quiz.explanation:
Monochromatic means consisting of one color — varying shades and tints of a single hue.
• monochromatic = using or involving only one color
• From Greek: 'mono-' (one) + 'khroma' (color)
• Distinct from achromatic (no color) — monochromatic has color, but only one

quiz.question: The therapist explained that anxiety can produce somatic symptoms like headaches and stomach pain. What does 'somatic' mean?
options: Relating to the mind, emotions, and psychological inner experience
Relating to the body, as distinct from the mind
Relating specifically to the central and peripheral nervous system
Relating to sleep, rest cycles, and the physical process of recovery
quiz.answer: Relating to the body, as distinct from the mind
quiz.explanation:
Somatic means of or relating to the body — as opposed to the mind or psyche.
• somatic = bodily; relating to the physical body
• From Greek: 'soma' — body
• 'Somatic symptoms' = physical symptoms arising from psychological causes

quiz.question: A numismatic collector attended the auction, outbidding everyone for a rare Roman coin. What does she collect?
options: Antique furniture and silverware
Rare stamps and postal history
Coins, banknotes, and medals
Antique maps and manuscripts
quiz.answer: Coins, banknotes, and medals
quiz.explanation:
Numismatic means relating to the study or collection of coins, banknotes, and medals.
• numismatic = of or relating to numismatics — the study of currency
• From Latin: 'numisma' — coin
• A numismatist is a coin collector or specialist

quiz.question: The sports fanatic painted his face, arrived four hours early, and refused to leave even in the pouring rain. What does 'fanatic' describe?
options: A casual supporter who attends a few games each season when available
A person with an extreme, obsessive devotion to something
A professional hired specifically to promote the team in the stands
A sports journalist covering the match for a national newspaper
quiz.answer: A person with an extreme, obsessive devotion to something
quiz.explanation:
A fanatic is a person with an extreme and uncritical enthusiasm or devotion to something.
• fanatic = excessively enthusiastic; obsessively devoted
• From Latin: 'fanaticus' — inspired by a god, frenzied
• 'Fan' is a shortening of 'fanatic'

quiz.question: The property market had been static for over a year. What does this tell us about prices?
options: Prices had been rising sharply and consistently month after month
Prices had been falling steadily throughout the year
Prices had barely changed — they were fixed in place
Prices had become erratic and completely unpredictable for buyers
quiz.answer: Prices had barely changed — they were fixed in place
quiz.explanation:
Static means showing little or no movement, change, or activity.
• static = lacking movement or development; stationary
• From Greek: 'statikos' — causing to stand
• 'Static electricity' is another sense — charge at rest, not flowing

quiz.question: The nature reserve was created to protect aquatic species. Which organisms were being protected?
options: Plants and animals living in deserts
Animals that live underground
Creatures that live in or near water
Species that live in forested mountain areas
quiz.answer: Creatures that live in or near water
quiz.explanation:
Aquatic means living or taking place in water.
• aquatic = of, in, or relating to water
• From Latin: 'aquaticus' — from 'aqua' (water)
• Aquatic animals include fish, amphibians, and aquatic mammals like dolphins

quiz.question: The critic said the film was too didactic — it never showed, only told. What was the criticism?
options: The film was too subtle, leaving most audiences thoroughly confused
The film was overly preachy, moralizing instead of trusting the story
The film had no coherent message or theme at all
The film relied too heavily on expensive special effects and action sequences
quiz.answer: The film was overly preachy, moralizing instead of trusting the story
quiz.explanation:
Didactic means designed to teach or moralize — and often implies doing so in a heavy-handed way.
• didactic = intended to instruct; often pejoratively, over-preachy
• From Greek: 'didaktikos' — apt at teaching
• 'A didactic novel' lectures the reader; it doesn't trust the story to carry the message

quiz.question: After an intense workout, lactic acid built up in the runner's muscles. What does 'lactic' relate to?
options: The distribution and efficiency of oxygen delivery through the bloodstream
Milk-related compounds or processes involving milk sugar fermentation
The transmission of electrical signals between individual muscle cells
The filtration and removal of metabolic waste products by the kidneys
quiz.answer: Milk-related compounds or processes involving milk sugar fermentation
quiz.explanation:
Lactic means relating to milk — lactic acid is produced by the fermentation of lactose.
• lactic = of or relating to milk
• From Latin: 'lac' (gen. 'lactis') — milk
• Lactic acid is produced in muscles during anaerobic exercise — the same compound made in yogurt fermentation

quiz.question: The chess player's winning tactic was to sacrifice her rook to expose the king. What is a tactic?
options: A comprehensive long-term strategic plan spanning many carefully plotted moves
A specific action or maneuver used to achieve an immediate goal
A fortunate mistake that accidentally ended up turning into an advantage
A defensive holding position designed primarily to avoid losing any more pieces
quiz.answer: A specific action or maneuver used to achieve an immediate goal
quiz.explanation:
A tactic is a specific method or action used to achieve a particular short-term objective.
• tactic = a planned action or method used to achieve a goal
• From Greek: 'taktike' — the art of arrangement
• Strategy is the overall plan; tactics are the specific actions that carry it out

quiz.question: The ER nurse described her twelve-hour shift as hectic. What was her shift like?
options: Long but remarkably quiet, with plenty of time to rest between patients
Full of frantic, non-stop activity and little chance to rest
Tediously slow, uneventful, and frustrating in its lack of any real challenge
Extremely well-organized and running efficiently from start to finish
quiz.answer: Full of frantic, non-stop activity and little chance to rest
quiz.explanation:
Hectic means full of incessant or frenzied activity — rushed, busy, and exhausting.
• hectic = characterized by feverish, unrelenting activity
• From Greek: 'hektikos' — habitual (originally used of a persistent fever)
• 'A hectic schedule', 'a hectic day' — the defining quality is breathless busyness

quiz.question: The DJ's eclectic playlist moved from 1960s soul to drum and bass to classical violin in the space of an hour. What does 'eclectic' mean here?
options: The playlist followed a strict historical order
It drew from a wide variety of very different sources and styles
It featured only one genre but many artists
It was designed to appeal to the most mainstream audience possible
quiz.answer: It drew from a wide variety of very different sources and styles
quiz.explanation:
Eclectic means deriving ideas, style, or taste from a broad and diverse range of sources.
• eclectic = selecting from various sources; varied and wide-ranging in origin
• From Greek: 'eklektikos' — selecting
• An eclectic person or collection is defined by diversity, not specialization

quiz.question: The expedition was equipped for arctic conditions. What kind of environment were they preparing for?
options: A hot, dry, arid desert environment with extreme daytime heat
An extremely cold environment in the far north
A hot, humid tropical jungle with high rainfall and dense vegetation
A high-altitude mountain zone with dangerously thin air and strong winds
quiz.answer: An extremely cold environment in the far north
quiz.explanation:
Arctic means relating to the North Pole region — extremely cold.
• arctic = of or relating to the Arctic (North Pole region); bitterly cold
• From Greek: 'arktikos' — of the Bear (the constellation Ursa Major, visible in the north)
• Lowercase 'arctic conditions' = any extremely cold, harsh environment

quiz.question: The Antarctic research station was the most remote outpost on Earth. Where was it located?
options: On the high plateau of central Asia
In the frozen wilderness around the North Pole
On or near the continent at the South Pole
On a remote island in the South Atlantic
quiz.answer: On or near the continent at the South Pole
quiz.explanation:
Antarctic means relating to the South Pole or the continent of Antarctica.
• antarctic = of or relating to the Antarctic (South Pole region)
• From Greek: 'anti-' (opposite) + 'arktikos' (of the north) — the opposite of the Arctic
• Antarctica is the continent; the Arctic is an ocean surrounded by land

quiz.question: The monk lived an ascetic life — one small meal a day, no possessions, and hours of silent prayer. What is an ascetic life?
options: A life of luxury and sensory pleasure treated as a form of spiritual celebration
A life of strict self-denial and abstinence for spiritual reasons
A life devoted entirely to scholarly study, debate, and intellectual inquiry
A life of active, outward charity and direct service within the local community
quiz.answer: A life of strict self-denial and abstinence for spiritual reasons
quiz.explanation:
Ascetic means practicing severe self-discipline and abstaining from all forms of indulgence.
• ascetic = characterized by severe self-denial; abstaining from pleasure
• From Greek: 'asketes' — monk, one who practices spiritual exercises
• An ascetic rejects physical pleasure not out of poverty but as a spiritual discipline
